criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the Generalized Solver (GS) parameterization, including the extended linear multistep signature, timestep schedule cumprod transformation, and additive coefficient corrections.

The plan includes implementing the extended solver signature that incorporates weighted sums of all previous points and all previous velocity directions (not just a K-step window), as defined in Equation 13.

The plan includes the cumprod-based timestep schedule parameterization where logits θ_n are passed through a sigmoid ('stick-breaking') and multiplied cumulatively to produce timesteps t_n^θ as defined in Equation 14.

The plan includes the three trainable parameter sets: θ for the timestep schedule, ϕ for solver coefficient additive corrections, and ξ for decoupled timestep corrections applied to diffusion model evaluations.

The extended GS signature (Equation 13) is implemented in code: each step x_{n+1} is computed as a weighted sum of all previous x_j values plus a weighted sum of all previous velocity evaluations v(x_j, t_j^θ + ξ_j).
The cumprod timestep schedule parameterization (Equation 14) is implemented: logits θ_n → sigmoid → cumulative product → t_n^θ = (T - δ) * prod(σ(θ_j), j=1..n) + δ.

The additive correction parameterization for the current-point coefficient is implemented as in Equation 15: a_{n,n}(θ, ϕ) = a_{n,n}(t_{n:n+1}^θ) + â_{n,n}(ϕ), where the theoretical coefficient from DPM-Solver++(3M) serves as guidance and a trainable scalar â_{n,n}(ϕ) is added.
The coefficient for 'old' velocities (more than K steps before) is implemented as a purely trained scalar ĉ_{j,n}(ϕ) with no theoretical term, as specified in Equation 16.
The coefficients for 'recent' velocities (within the K-step window) are implemented using additive corrections to the theoretical finite-difference-based terms, as defined in Equations 17–19.
The decoupled timestep correction ξ is implemented: the diffusion model is evaluated at t_j^θ + ξ_j (decoupled from the step timestep t_j^θ), as in Equation 20.
All trainable correction parameters (ξ, â_{j,n}(ϕ), ĉ_{j,n}(ϕ)) are initialized to zero so that the GS initially reduces exactly to the base theoretical solver (DPM-Solver++(3M)).

The full Generalized Solver step matches Algorithm 1 from Appendix D.3: the first step uses Euler, second step uses one finite-difference correction D1_0, and steps n≥2 use both first- and second-order corrections D1 and D2, plus the summed historical x_j and v_j terms.

The agent has read Section 3.2 to understand the GAS training objective: combining distillation loss L_distill with adversarial loss L_adv using the relativistic GAN formulation and gradient penalties.
The plan includes implementing the GAS combined objective: min_{θ,ϕ,ξ} max_ψ [L_distill(θ,ϕ,ξ) + L_adv(θ,ϕ,ξ,ψ)], where student and teacher use different independent noise samples x_T and y_T.
The distillation loss L_distill is implemented as LPIPS in pixel-space experiments and L1 in latent-space experiments, as stated in Section 3.2 and Appendix D.4.

The adversarial loss L_adv is implemented using the relativistic GAN loss (Equation 22): f(D_ψ(Φ^S(x_T)) - D_ψ(Φ^T(y_T))) with f(t) = -log(1 + e^{-t}), where x_T and y_T are independently sampled noise vectors.

The discriminator gradient penalty (Equation 11) is implemented with penalties on both real (teacher) and fake (student) outputs: λ_1 * E[||∇_x D_ψ(x)||²] + λ_2 * E[||∇_x D_ψ(G(z))||²], with λ_1 = λ_2 = 0.1.

The R3GAN discriminator architecture from Huang et al. (2024) is used: pretrained checkpoint for pixel-space experiments (CIFAR10 and FFHQ-64 checkpoints for respective datasets), and randomly initialized FFHQ-64 architecture for latent-space experiments.

For the Stable Diffusion discriminator, the first convolution layer of the R3GAN FFHQ-64 architecture is modified to accept 4-channel latent inputs, and the discriminator is trained from scratch in latent space.
EMA (Exponential Moving Average) of solver parameters is implemented with decay = 0.999, and EMA weights are used for evaluation; EMA is updated after each training iteration.
The agent has read Section 4 and Appendix D to understand all experimental settings: datasets, pretrained models, teacher configurations, optimizer settings, and training durations.
The plan includes pixel-space experiments on CIFAR10 (32×32), FFHQ (64×64), and AFHQv2 (64×64) using EDM pretrained models with UniPC (order=3, NFE=20, logSNR schedule) as the teacher.

The plan includes latent-space experiments on LSUN-Bedroom (256×256) and ImageNet-256 using LDM pretrained models, with UniPC teachers (NFE=20, time-uniform for LSUN; NFE=10, time-quadratic for ImageNet).

The plan includes the text-to-image experiment on MS-COCO (512×512) using Stable Diffusion v1.5, with 'one-plus' teacher strategy (teacher NFE = student NFE + 1) using IPNDM(2M) solver with GITS schedule.

The teacher dataset generation follows the UniPC parameters from Table 17: order=3, NFE=20/10, appropriate time schedules and B(h) variants per dataset, with t_eps=1e-4 for pixel-space and 1e-3 for latent-space.

Training hyperparameters for pixel-space experiments are correctly implemented: solver optimizer Adam (lr=0.001, betas=(0.9,0.999), weight_decay=0.0), discriminator Adam (lr=0.00001), gradient clipping by norm=1.0, batch_size=24, 10k iterations.

Training hyperparameters for latent-space experiments are correctly implemented: batch_size=8 for both solver and discriminator training, Adam optimizers with same lr/beta settings as pixel-space, gradient clipping by norm=1.0, 30k iterations.
Adversarial loss weights are correctly set per dataset: weight=0.1 for CIFAR10 and weight=1.0 for FFHQ, AFHQv2, LSUN-Bedroom, ImageNet, and MS-COCO experiments.
Training and validation dataset sizes are correctly configured: 1400/1000 for GS on pixel-space datasets; 5000/1000 for GAS on FFHQ and AFHQv2; 5000/1000 for GAS on latent-space datasets.

FID evaluation is computed using 50,000 randomly generated samples for all experiments except MS-COCO (30,000 samples), using EMA model weights and reference statistics from Karras et al. (2022) for pixel-space.
GS and GAS training runs on pixel-space datasets (CIFAR10, FFHQ, AFHQv2) execute successfully without errors for NFE ∈ {4, 6, 8, 10}.
GS and GAS training runs on latent-space datasets (LSUN-Bedroom, ImageNet-256) execute successfully without errors for NFE ∈ {4, 5, 6, 7}.
GS and GAS training runs on MS-COCO with Stable Diffusion v1.5 execute successfully without errors for NFE ∈ {4, 5, 6, 7}.
The results on CIFAR10 match Table 2a: GAS achieves FID ≤ 4.05 at NFE=4 and FID ≤ 2.49 at NFE=6, outperforming S4S Alt (6.35 and 2.67 respectively).
The results on FFHQ match Table 2a: GAS achieves FID ≤ 7.86 at NFE=4 and FID ≤ 3.79 at NFE=6, outperforming S4S Alt (10.63 and 4.62 respectively).
The results on AFHQv2 match Table 2a: GAS achieves FID ≤ 4.48 at NFE=4 and FID ≤ 2.66 at NFE=6, outperforming S4S Alt (6.52 and 2.70 respectively).
The results on LSUN-Bedroom match Table 2b: GAS achieves FID ≤ 6.68 at NFE=4 and FID ≤ 4.60 at NFE=5, outperforming Best LD3 (8.48 and 5.93 respectively).
The results on ImageNet-256 match Table 2b: GAS achieves FID ≤ 5.38 at NFE=4, outperforming Best LD3 (9.19) and matching S4S Alt† (5.13).
The agent has read Section 4.2 and Table 3 to understand the parameterization ablation comparing GS against the S4S LMS+PC variant under identical hyperparameters and training budget.

The parameterization ablation is planned and implemented: GS versus S4S LMS+PC are trained with identical batch size (24), same 49k training dataset, and evaluated by both FID and LPIPS on CIFAR10 and FFHQ.

The parameterization ablation experiment executes successfully and results match Table 3: GS achieves substantially better FID than S4S at NFE=4 (4.41 vs 31.44 on CIFAR10; 10.79 vs 24.24 on FFHQ) and better LPIPS across all NFE settings.
The agent has read Appendix B.1 to understand the theoretical guidance ablation: GS trained with and without theoretical coefficient guidance on FFHQ, initialized to DPM-Solver++(3M).
The theoretical guidance ablation is implemented: GS is trained with and without the additive theoretical guidance from DPM-Solver++(3M) on FFHQ, and FID is evaluated for NFE ∈ {4, 6, 8, 10}.
The theoretical guidance ablation results match Table 5: adding theoretical guidance substantially reduces FID (e.g., 10.70 vs 15.23 at NFE=4, and 4.49 vs 10.53 at NFE=6 on FFHQ).
The agent has read Appendix B.2 to understand the GAN loss comparison: relativistic vs traditional GAN loss for GAS on ImageNet at NFE=4.
The adversarial loss ablation is implemented: GAS with relativistic loss (Equation 10) versus standard GAN loss (Equation 9) are compared on ImageNet at NFE=4 using the same gradient penalty setup.
The adversarial loss comparison results match Table 6: relativistic GAS achieves FID=5.38, outperforming traditional GAS (6.49) and GS without adversarial loss (7.87) on ImageNet at NFE=4.

The agent has read Section 4.3 and Appendix C.1 to understand the dataset size efficiency experiment: GS convergence with 1400 samples vs 49000, and GAS requiring 5000 samples for FFHQ and higher-dimensional datasets.
The dataset size ablation experiment is implemented and run for GS with dataset sizes 1400 and 49000 on CIFAR10 and FFHQ, and for GAS with sizes 1400, 5000, and 49000 on FFHQ.

Dataset size ablation results match Table 12: GS with 1400 samples achieves equivalent FID to 49000 samples (e.g., 4.35 vs 4.39 at NFE=4 CIFAR10); GAS with 5000 samples matches 49000-sample performance on FFHQ.
The agent has read Appendix B.4 to understand the cross-dataset generalization experiment: GS and GAS trained on FFHQ applied to AFHQv2 and vice versa.
The cross-dataset generalization experiment is implemented: GS and GAS checkpoints trained on FFHQ are applied to AFHQv2 (and vice versa), with FID compared against baselines.
Cross-dataset generalization results match Table 7: transferred GS' and GAS' (trained on FFHQ, applied on AFHQv2) outperform DPM-Solver++, LD3, and S4S baselines on AFHQv2 across all NFE.
The agent has read Appendix B.5 and Figure 6 to understand that GAS adversarial loss weight sensitivity shows stable FID across a wide range (0.2 to 500) on FFHQ at NFE=4.
The adversarial loss weight sweep experiment is implemented on FFHQ at NFE=4, testing multiple weight values including 0 (=GS), 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0.
The agent has read Appendix B.6 and Table 8 to understand the mode collapse analysis using precision, recall, density, and coverage metrics on ImageNet at NFE=4.

The mode collapse analysis is implemented: precision, recall, density, and coverage from Naeem et al. (2020) are computed for GS and GAS on ImageNet at NFE=4, comparing 50000 generated samples to 50000 teacher images using T4096.

Mode collapse results match Table 8: GAS achieves higher recall (0.76 vs 0.63) and coverage (0.97 vs 0.90) than GS, and equal or higher precision and density, while also achieving better FID (5.38 vs 7.87).
The agent has read Appendix B.7 and Table 9 to understand the comparison with Consistency Distillation (CD) in terms of FID and coverage on CIFAR10.

The comparison with Consistency Distillation (CD) is run using the official CD checkpoint with ternary search at NFE=4 on CIFAR10, measuring FID and coverage against 10000 generated images and the CIFAR10 test set using T4096.
CD comparison results match Table 9: GS and GAS achieve higher coverage than CD (0.963 and 0.961 vs 0.938) at NFE=4 on CIFAR10, demonstrating better generative property preservation.
The agent has read Appendix B.9 and Table 10 to understand the parameterization comparison of GS against S4S LMS and S4S LMS+PC under identical training configurations on FFHQ.
The S4S LMS and LMS+PC parameterizations are implemented within the same codebase as GS, with identical hyperparameters (from Appendix D.4.1) and initial DPM-Solver++(3M) coefficient values.
Results match Table 10: GS outperforms both S4S LMS (17.05 vs 10.70 at NFE=4) and S4S LMS+PC (45.51 vs 10.70 at NFE=4) on FFHQ across all NFE settings.

Adversarial training ablation on FFHQ and LSUN-Bedroom at NFE=4 matches Table 4: GAS achieves better FID (7.86 vs 10.70 on FFHQ; 7.54 vs 9.64 on LSUN) despite slightly higher L_distill compared to GS.
Inference time for GS on ImageNet (1024 images, batch 64, single H100) matches Table 16: approximately 0.36m for NFE=4, 0.45m for NFE=5, 0.55m for NFE=6, 0.64m for NFE=7 — comparable to UniPC.
